topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: Hey, I'm struggling with how to properly expose a Rust library to C when we're doing a code review on the new FFI module. The current setup feels messy and I can't seem to get the header generation right.

That's totally understandable, moving from Java to systems programming with Rust has some steep learning curves for C interop. Have you looked at how cbindgen handles the configuration in your cbindgen.toml file?
I've been looking at it but I'm not sure which fields matter most for our use case. We need to export a bunch of functions and types without too much noise.

Great question. For cbindgen.toml, you really want to focus on the visibility settings and the output format options. The key is making sure your pub extern C fns are properly annotated so that the header generation produces exactly what your Java side needs.
So if I annotate my functions with pub extern C, does cbindgen automatically pick those up in the configuration?
Exactly. As long as you're using Rust types and traits correctly, cbindgen will scan your module for items marked as pub extern C and include them in the generated output.
I'm still worried about documentation propagation though. How do I ensure that my Java team gets up-to-date docs?
You need to make sure your Rust doc comments are properly formatted so they propagate into the generated headers. cbindgen respects those annotations and includes them in the header files.
That makes sense, but what about CI validation? I want to catch any mismatches before we merge.

Perfect call. You should add a CI step that runs cbindgen on your codebase and compares the generated headers against a golden file or expected output. That way you'll catch any drift from changes to your Rust types and traits.
And what about the header generation itself? Does it handle enums properly?
It does, but you might want to tweak the configuration options in cbindgen.toml for specific enum handling. Just make sure your Rust types and traits are aligned with how you expect C to treat them.
Ah, I see now. It's not just about writing the code but configuring it right so cbindgen does what we want without manual intervention every time.
That's a great summary. The whole point is to automate as much of this as possible with cbindgen and your build system.
Wait, one more thing - how do I verify that the exported functions actually match what Java expects when we call them from a C layer?
You can use cbindgen's output to generate test cases that validate the signature and behavior. Just make sure your Rust types and traits are consistent across all layers.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: Imagine I'm working on a high-performance game server where we need to export memory-safe Rust logic into a legacy C++ binding layer. We have a critical production issue right now where our FFI boundary isn't generating the correct C headers, and I suspect something is wrong with how our traits are configured.

That sounds like a tight deadline scenario. The most common cause for this exact problem is that cbindgen relies on the Rust compiler to emit documentation for functions you intend to export, and if those functions lack the right annotations or visibility modifiers, the generated C header will be incomplete or incorrect. Think of it like trying to build a bridge without blueprints; even if you have the materials (the Rust code), if you don't mark which sections are public and safe for external traffic, the structure won't hold up under inspection.

I've tried adding pub extern C to my functions in lib.rs but the output still looks wrong. I feel like maybe I need to manually edit the generated header files instead of relying on cbindgen, which seems risky for a production system.

You are absolutely right to be cautious about manual edits; modifying generated headers directly is never the right path because any future update will overwrite your changes. The solution lies in configuring cbindgen.toml correctly so that it knows exactly which functions and traits to include. Specifically, you must ensure every function marked for export uses the trait impl Serialize or similar traits if you want documentation propagation, but more importantly, the build system needs to parse lib.rs with the correct compiler flags enabled.
I see. So I shouldn't be touching the header files at all? Can cbindgen really handle complex types like our custom structs and enums without me having to write extra manual documentation comments?

Exactly, that is the beauty of using cbindgen with proper configuration. You don't need to manually document every field or enum variant; as long as you implement the necessary traits on your types and ensure the build process runs cbindgen against lib.rs, it will automatically generate accurate C headers for your structs and enums. The system acts like a compiler that reads your source code and produces documentation in another format, rather than relying on human-written comments.
Wait, I thought cbindgen only works with simple functions? What happens if I have complex generics or unsafe blocks inside my exported functions?

You can handle complex generics and safe code fine, but you must avoid unsafe blocks unless they are explicitly marked as part of the FFI boundary. If your function contains an unsafe block that isn't intended to be exposed to C, cbindgen will skip it or generate invalid warnings because the C side cannot know how to manage memory safety without explicit instructions. The key is ensuring only the safe, documented parts of your logic are annotated for export.
Okay, so I need to update my cbindgen.toml file and maybe run a specific build command? How do I verify that the generated headers actually match what our C++ side expects?

Yes, you must configure cbindgen.toml to specify the output directory and ensure your Cargo build script runs it as a dependency. To verify correctness, you should run the generated headers through a CI validation step that compares them against your expected template or manually diff them before merging into production. This ensures that even if your Rust code changes slightly, the C interface remains stable.

That makes sense for stability. But what about when our game engine updates and we need to re-export a trait? Does cbindgen automatically handle new trait implementations or do I have to manually update the config?

cbindgen will automatically pick up any new traits you implement in lib.rs if they are marked as public and annotated. However, if you add a new trait that isn't yet configured in cbindgen.toml to be exported, it won't appear in the C header until you update the config file. Think of cbindgen like an API spec generator; it only documents what you tell it to document via configuration.
I'm worried about timing here since we're under pressure. If I make a mistake in my config, will the build fail completely or just produce an incorrect header?

If your configuration is invalid, cbindgen will output error messages to stderr explaining what was wrong with the syntax or missing fields. It won't silently produce a broken header unless you ignore those warnings. This means you can detect issues immediately during the build phase rather than finding them later at runtime when the FFI breaks.
Great, so I should add some error handling to catch these build failures early? And what about performance impact on our game server?

Yes, integrating the cbindgen command into your CI pipeline with a timeout will help you catch errors before they reach production. As for performance, generating headers is negligible compared to runtime execution of your actual game logic; the overhead is measured in milliseconds during the build process, not during gameplay.
One last thing: if I accidentally expose a function that shouldn't be public to C, will cbindgen just ignore it or generate garbage?

If you forget the pub keyword on a function you intended for export, cbindgen simply won't include it in the generated header. It acts like a filter that only passes functions with the correct visibility modifiers. To ensure you don't accidentally expose internal logic, always double-check your annotations before committing to production.
Thanks for the clear explanation on traits and config. I think I'm ready to start configuring cbindgen.toml now. Just want to confirm one more thing: do I need to run rustfmt before running cbindgen?

While it is not strictly required, running rustfmt first ensures your code follows standard formatting conventions which helps the compiler parse documentation comments correctly. It's a good habit to keep your source code clean so that tools like cbindgen can interpret your intent without ambiguity.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: I'm starting a new job and need to set up the FFI layer for our C API using cbindgen. I thought the easiest way was just to let cbindgen generate everything automatically without any manual configuration in cbindgen.toml.

That's a common first assumption, but it won't work for most real projects. You can't skip configuring pub extern C fns entirely; you need to explicitly annotate the functions you want exported in your Rust code and then configure cbindgen.toml to match those annotations. The tool respects the #[doc] attribute on those specific items so it knows what to include.
Okay, I see. So I should just add pub extern C to my functions and then cbindgen will pick them up? What exactly do I put in the config file?

You define the Rust items with pub extern C attributes, then in your cbindgen.toml you specify the output directory and optionally filter which crate or module to process. You can also set language targets like C99 or C11 depending on what your downstream clients need.
Got it, but I'm worried about documentation. Will cbindgen automatically pull the doc comments from my Rust code into the generated header?

Yes, as long as your #[doc] attribute is properly formatted and placed on the pub extern C function or struct it wraps. The tool propagates that documentation into the generated .h file so your API docs stay in sync with the code.
That's helpful. One more thing though, I want to make sure cbindgen actually runs every time we build our project. How do I integrate this into CI?

You add a step in your pipeline that invokes the build command with cbindgen enabled as an argument, then compare the generated headers against a snapshot or run a diff tool to catch accidental regressions. Many teams use this exact pattern to validate FFI stability.
Perfect. But what if I later want to switch from cbindgen to a different header generator? Is the setup fragile?

It's not inherently fragile, but you do need to keep your Rust annotations consistent with whatever tool you choose. If you move away from cbindgen, you'll have to re-implement the same pub extern C pattern for your new generator.
I understand now. Just one last question: does cbindgen handle complex types like Option or Result in the header?
Yes, but you may need to use #[doc = "..."] with specific keywords to control how those types appear. Sometimes it generates verbose boilerplate unless you guide it explicitly.

topic: Build - cbindgen for Exporting Rust to C

turns.content: I've read about cbindgen and how it uses the serde_json::Value format internally, but I still don't get why my generated header is missing the doc comments even though I added them. I feel like I'm just guessing at syntax.

It's frustrating when you think you did everything right but the output doesn't match your expectations. The core issue here isn't about the format itself, but how cbindgen processes the crate. You need to ensure every function intended for export is annotated with #[cfg_attr(feature = "c", doc(""). This tells the parser to include that string in the generated header. If you omit this annotation on a public extern C function, it simply won't appear in the documentation output.
